title: Tiling a bathroom wall
subtitle: Lay a full run of ceramic tiles on a wet-area wall
excerpt: A step-by-step guide to setting out, cutting, and fixing ceramic wall tiles in a bathroom: finding the datum line, mixing and applying adhesive, cutting around obstacles, grouting, and sealing the perimeter.
sourceNotes: British Standards BS 5385-1: Wall and floor tiling. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s). City and Guilds Level 2 Tiling syllabus.

content.text: Tiling a bathroom wall is a two-session job: adhesive one day, grout the next. The work breaks into four stages: preparation, setting out, laying, and finishing. Getting the set-out right before any adhesive is mixed determines whether the finished wall looks considered or rushed. A correctly set bathroom wall has balanced cut tiles at top and bottom and full tiles in the most visible run.
What you need
Materials
Ceramic wall tiles.
Buy 10 per cent more than the net wall area to cover cuts and breakages. Standard bathroom tile sizes are 200 x 250 mm, 250 x 400 mm, and 300 x 600 mm.
Wet-area tile adhesive
. Ready-mixed or powder-and-water, white or grey. 5 kg covers roughly 2.5 square metres at 3 mm bed thickness.
Tile spacers
. 2 mm for most bathrooms; 3 mm if the tiles are hand-made or less precise in size.
Waterproof grout
. White, grey, or coloured. One 2.5 kg bag covers approximately 5 square metres at a 2 mm joint.
Silicone sealant (sanitary-grade, anti-mould). For the perimeter joints where tiles meet the bath, shower tray, and internal corners.
Grout float and sponge. A rubber-faced grout float presses grout into the joints; a damp sponge removes the surplus.
Tools
Tape measure, pencil, spirit level, and chalk line for setting out.
Notched adhesive spreader (a trowel with a serrated 6 mm V-notch for wall tiles, 10 mm for large-format tiles).
Tile scorer and tile nipper or a wet tile saw. See the companion technique tutorial on cutting tiles.
Cordless drill with a mixing paddle attachment for powder adhesive.
Step 1, prepare the wall

Tiles bond to a flat, sound, clean surface. Remove any existing tiles with a bolster chisel or electric chisel. Make good any holes or depressions to within 3 mm of flat using patching plaster; a surface that undulates by more than 3 mm will show as uneven grout joints once tiled. Allow repairs to cure fully before tiling. Sand down any high spots or adhesive ridges left by the previous tiles. The wall should be structurally dry: if the plaster feels cold and clammy, allow a further 48 hours before starting.
Step 2, set out the datum line
Find the lowest point of the bath rim or shower tray with a spirit level. Measure up one full tile height plus 3 mm from the lowest point and draw a level horizontal line across the wall. This is the
datum line

. The first full course of tiles will sit on this line, and all the cut tiles will be between the datum and the bath or tray. Fix a straight timber batten (a length of 50 x 25 mm timber sawn square) along the datum line with two or three masonry screws into wall plugs. This batten supports the first course while the adhesive sets and stops tiles sliding before the adhesive grabs.

Next, set out the vertical centre. Find the horizontal centre of the wall and mark a plumb vertical line. Hold a row of tiles along the batten including spacers and check how the end cuts fall. If a cut tile at each side would be narrower than half a tile, shift the centre by half a tile width and re-mark the vertical. Aim for equal cut tiles at each side that are each at least one-third of a full tile wide.
Step 3, mix the adhesive

Mix powder adhesive to a smooth, creamy consistency with no lumps. It should hold a notch cleanly when the spreader is drawn through it. Ready-mixed adhesive is used straight from the tub. Work in areas of no more than 1 square metre at a time; adhesive skins over in 20 to 30 minutes in a warm room and tiled-over skinned adhesive will fail.
Step 4, fix the tiles

Apply adhesive to the wall in an area of about 600 x 600 mm using the flat face of the spreader, then comb it into parallel ridges with the notched edge. Press the first tile onto the batten at the centre vertical line, twisting it slightly as it goes on to collapse the adhesive ridges. Full coverage of the back face is the goal; lift the tile and check after the first few: the adhesive should coat more than 80 per cent of the back. Push spacers into the corners of the tile. Work outward from the centre, placing a full horizontal course before moving up. Check level every four or five tiles; a tile slightly out of level compounds across a row.

Measure and cut the tiles for the perimeter. Do not attempt to cut the perimeter tiles before all the full tiles are laid: the actual gap is the measurement that matters, not the theoretical one. Mark each cut tile individually, score, and snap (or cut on the wet saw). See the cutting technique tutorial for pipe and socket cut-outs.
Step 5, remove the batten and tile the bottom row

Allow the adhesive to cure for the time stated on the packaging (typically 24 hours before grouting, 4 to 6 hours before light handling). Remove the support batten and unscrew it. Measure the gap between the bottom of the first course and the bath rim and cut the bottom row of tiles. These cut tiles sit in the most concealed position on the wall; a gap of 2 mm between the tile and the bath edge is correct, as the silicone joint will fill it. Do not use adhesive or grout in this perimeter joint.
Step 6, grout

Remove all the plastic spacers before grouting (some spacers are left in and grouted over; check the product instructions). Mix the grout to a stiff paste. Apply it diagonally across the tiles with the grout float, pressing it firmly into the joints. Work in areas of about 1 square metre. After 10 to 15 minutes, wipe off the surplus with a barely damp sponge, rinsing the sponge frequently. Do not use a soaking-wet sponge: excess water weakens the grout. Allow the grout to haze over, then polish with a dry cloth. Full grout cure takes 24 hours; do not wet the wall for 24 hours.
Step 7, seal the perimeter joints

The joint between the lowest tile course and the bath rim, and all internal corner joints where two tiled walls meet, must be sealed with sanitary-grade silicone, not grout. Grout cracks at these joints because the bath and the wall move independently. Apply masking tape 2 mm from the edge on each surface, run a continuous bead of silicone into the joint, smooth with a wetted finger, and remove the tape while the silicone is still wet. Allow 24 hours before exposing to water.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Use a stiffer mix. Check and warm the room to above 10°C. Allow the wall to dry further. Add a temporary support batten under each course while the adhesive grabs.
attrs.cause: Adhesive too wet, or applied to a cold or damp wall. The surface temperature is below 5°C.
attrs.symptom: Tiles slip down the wall before the adhesive grabs

attrs.fix: Always place spacers at all four corners. Use a straight-edge or spirit level to check the alignment of joint lines every few courses.
attrs.cause: Spacers placed inconsistently or tiles pressed hard against adjacent tiles, compressing the spacer on one side.
attrs.symptom: Grout joint widths vary across the wall

attrs.fix: Remove the failed silicone with a silicone remover. Clean the bath rim and tile face with methylated spirit. Allow to dry completely before applying fresh silicone.
attrs.cause: Silicone applied over grout or adhesive residue, or the bath surface was not cleaned and dried before sealing.
attrs.symptom: Silicone bead peels away from the bath within a year

attrs.fix: Hollow tiles must be removed and re-laid. Tap gently with a rubber mallet to see if the tile can be released. Apply fresh adhesive with full coverage. Large areas of hollow tiles indicate an adhesive compatibility problem: check the adhesive specification for the tile type and background.
attrs.cause: Insufficient adhesive coverage on the back of the tile, or a skinned adhesive bed that bonded to the face of the tile but not fully to the wall.
attrs.symptom: Tiles hollow when tapped after the adhesive has set
